text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE / MOLECULAR IMAGING REPORT

EXAMINATION: 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT, whole body with skull vertex to mid-thigh, supplemented by skull survey for intracranial assessment

CLINICAL INDICATION: 72-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant). Prior treatments include leup (ongoing), abi (discontinued at CRPC), definitive EBRT (78 Gy to prostate/pelvis), Xtandi (discontinued at bone progression), Lynparza (discontinued for visceral progression/transfusion-dependent anemia). CT-biopsy confirmed hepatic metastasis (segment VII) from prostate primary, without neuroendocrine transformation. Referred for PSMA PET/CT to characterize extent of disease and assess eligibility for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) radioligand therapy. Prior PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 24 months ago at time of CRPC declaration demonstrated right iliac chain nodal uptake and a single right posterior iliac crest osseous focus.

COMPARISON: Prior 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT (approximately 24 months prior). Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and whole-body bone scan (approximately 2 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous administration of 68Ga-PSMA-11 (185 MBq, confirmed activity), the patient rested comfortably for a 60-minute uptake period. PET/CT images were acquired from skull vertex to mid-femur in standard fashion. Low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic localization was obtained without intravenous contrast. Dedicated skull survey images were reviewed for intracranial involvement. Images reviewed on dedicated workstation with multiplanar reconstruction in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No abnormal PSMA-avid foci within the calvarium, skull base, or intracranial compartment. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular foci of uptake. Salivary gland and lacrimal gland physiologic uptake noted bilaterally, as expected with this tracer.

CHEST:
Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses with appreciable PSMA avidity. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy with pathologic PSMA uptake.

Bone (thoracic):
-- T10 vertebral body: Areas of sclerotic change corresponding to the previously irradiated metastatic lesion (30 Gy in 10 fractions, completed approximately 8 months prior). Residual heterogeneous PSMA uptake at T10, SUVmax 4.1, somewhat lower than the expected pre-treatment level, though meaningful uptake remains. No definitive epidural soft tissue extension. Post-radiation marrow changes are present.
-- T12 vertebral body: New compared to prior PSMA PET/CT. Focal PSMA-avid lesion in the left posterolateral T12 vertebral body, SUVmax 5.7, without cortical breakthrough or associated soft tissue mass. Not clearly seen on the prior bone scan performed approximately 2 months ago.
-- Right posterior 6th rib: Persistent PSMA-avid focus at the posterolateral right 6th rib, SUVmax 3.9. Previously noted on conventional bone scan approximately 6 months prior; stable in size and tracer avidity compared to that time.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: A 1.8 cm hypoenhancing lesion in hepatic segment VII demonstrates intense PSMA avidity (SUVmax 8.4). This is interval growth from the 1.4 cm lesion documented on CT approximately 8 weeks ago and confirmed as metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma on CT-guided core biopsy. No additional hepatic lesions are identified with confidence above background, though a subtle 0.5 cm area of mildly elevated uptake in segment III is noted (SUVmax 2.2) and is nonspecific -- may reflect very early micro-metastatic involvement versus background; correlation with follow-up imaging recommended.

Retroperitoneum / Lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the level of the aortic bifurcation with convincing PSMA avidity. Physiologic renal, hepatic, and splenic uptake noted.

Prostate bed / Pelvis:
-- Right iliac chain: The previously identified right iliac chain lymph node demonstrates persistent and now somewhat more prominent PSMA avidity (node measures approximately 1.3 cm on CT, SUVmax 6.1), representing interval increase in metabolic activity compared to the prior PSMA PET/CT where SUVmax was 4.2. Small satellite lymph node foci in the right external iliac chain also show low-level PSMA uptake (SUVmax 2.3-2.7), representing possible early nodal involvement.
-- Prostate bed: Heterogeneous post-treatment changes without a discrete PSMA-avid soft tissue mass to suggest gross local recurrence.

Bone (lumbar spine and pelvis):
-- L3 vertebral body: PSMA-avid lesion in the right hemivertebra of L3 (SUVmax 5.2). Previously identified on MRI spine as a focus of early marrow signal change.
-- Right posterior iliac crest: Persistent PSMA-avid osseous lesion (SUVmax 4.9), the index lesion first identified on PSMA PET/CT approximately 24 months ago. Modestly increased uptake compared to prior PET, consistent with ongoing disease activity.
-- Left iliac wing: Previously identified on conventional bone scan approximately 6 months prior. PSMA-avid focus at the left anterior iliac wing (SUVmax 4.4), corresponding to a subtle sclerotic focus on CT.
-- Right femoral head: New compared to all prior imaging. Focal PSMA-avid lesion in the right femoral head/neck junction (SUVmax 3.8), without cortical involvement or fracture risk feature on CT review. This represents a new site of osseous metastatic disease not identified on the bone scan performed approximately 2 months ago.

IMPRESSION:

1. Extensive PSMA-avid metastatic castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma involving multiple anatomic compartments, representing significant interval progression compared to PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 24 months ago.

2. Osseous disease: Seven discrete sites of PSMA-avid bone involvement identified -- T10 vertebral body (previously irradiated, residual uptake), T12 vertebral body (new), right posterior 6th rib (stable), L3 vertebral body (previously seen on MRI), right posterior iliac crest (index lesion, slight increase in uptake), left anterior iliac wing (previously seen on bone scan), and right femoral head/neck junction (new). The right femoral head lesion is a new finding and warrants orthopedic surgery or bone health clinic awareness given weight-bearing location, though no fracture or cortical breach is identified at this time.

3. Nodal disease: Right iliac chain nodal metastasis with increased PSMA avidity compared to prior. Additional small satellite right external iliac chain foci are noted with low-level uptake, possible early nodal involvement.

4. Visceral disease: PSMA-avid hepatic segment VII metastasis (SUVmax 8.4, 1.8 cm), with high tracer expression. A second small indeterminate focus in segment III (SUVmax 2.2) is nonspecific and requires follow-up. The segment VII lesion was biopsy-confirmed as prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine differentiation.

5. PSMA expression assessment: All identified metastatic sites demonstrate meaningful PSMA avidity (SUVmax range 3.8 to 8.4 across lesions). No lesion with appreciable FDG-avid, PSMA-low phenotype is identified to suggest significant neuroendocrine dedifferentiation or PSMA-low subclonal escape. These findings are consistent with high and relatively uniform PSMA expression across the metastatic burden. From a tracer-uptake standpoint, the tumor PSMA expression profile would appear favorable for consideration of PSMA-directed radioligand therapy, though clinical eligibility determination per institutional criteria is at the discretion of the treating team.

6. No intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease. No cord compression identified, though a dedicated MRI spine study (most recently performed approximately 6 months ago) should be considered for updated neurological risk assessment given new T12 involvement.

Reporting Physician: [Attending Nuclear Medicine / Molecular Imaging Physician]
Technologist: [Nuclear Medicine Technologist]
Radiopharmaceutical: 68Ga-PSMA-11, 185 MBq IV
Uptake time: 60 minutes
Electronically signed and finalized.
